title: Chapter 19: Two Ghosts
content: Three weeks in the Underlayer, and Wren learned the rhythm of invisibility.

Wake at dusk. The community moved on a nocturnal schedule — safer, quieter, fewer visible eyes to glitch around. Eat whatever the supply chain had left at the drop points. Check in with Margot at the main chamber, who tracked movements like a dispatcher running a ghost railroad.
Then disappear into the tunnels and do whatever it took to feel human.
For Wren, that meant drawing.

She'd found a sketchpad in one of the supply crates — a proper one, spiral-bound, sixty-pound paper. Someone had left it. Maybe intentionally, maybe not. She didn't care. The first time she put pencil to paper, her hand moved with a ferocity that surprised her. Lines, shapes, shadows — the Underlayer pouring out of her like water from a cracked dam.

She drew the tunnels. The cathedrals of rust and cable where the invisible gathered. The way light fell through grates onto the tracks below — thin and gold, like god-rays in a church nobody attended.

She drew the people. Keiko with her headphones, perpetually untethered. Margot with her scarred mouth and military posture. The man with the knife — Roland — whose hands never stopped moving, always cutting, sharpening, peeling something.
And Ash.

She drew Ash without meaning to. The angle of his shoulders against the wall. The way his scars caught the dark and turned it into something luminous. The hard line of his jaw and the softer place where his pulse beat beneath the neon tracery on his neck.
She drew him and drew him and drew him, and each time she told herself it was just practice, just anatomy, just the most interesting face in a room full of ghosts.
She was lying to herself. She knew it. She kept drawing anyway.
Ash kept his distance.

Not avoidance — not exactly. He was simply... economical. With his words, his time, his presence. He appeared when something needed doing and vanished when it didn't. He never lingered in the main chamber. Never joined the others for meals unless Margot demanded it.
But he was always there when Wren needed something.

The night she couldn't find the medical supply drop, he materialized at the end of the tunnel and walked her there without a word. The morning she woke with a fever and a cough that rattled her ribs, a bottle of antibiotics appeared outside her alcove. No note. No signature. Just the faint glow of neon scars retreating around the corner.
She watched him from across rooms. He watched her back.
Neither of them said anything about it.

The rooftop access vent was on the far east side of the Underlayer, where a maintenance ladder led up through a defunct ventilation shaft to a grate that opened onto a low roof above a Chinese grocery. It was the only place in the network where you could see the sky.

Wren found Ash there at 2 AM, sitting with his back against an HVAC unit, his forearms resting on his knees. His scars glowed faintly in the night air — brighter than they did underground, fed by the ambient electromagnetic hum of the city. The neon lines traced from his wrists to his elbows and up, disappearing beneath his sleeves, reappearing at his collarbones and throat.
She climbed out and sat next to him. Not close. Enough.

The city sprawled below them — lit windows, traffic streams, the distant glitter of bridges. Noise rose up in a sustained hum: sirens, horns, the bass-thump of a club seven blocks south. The air tasted like tar and rain and the metallic bite of the rooftop beneath her.
"First time up here?" he asked.
"Margot said there was a view. She wasn't lying."
"Mm." He looked out at the skyline. The neon on his neck pulsed once, twice — matching his heartbeat, Wren realized. The scars were tied to something deeper than skin.
She pulled out her sketchpad. A habit now — her hand found the pencil before her mind caught up.
"Do you ever want to go back?" she asked.
The question hung in the air between them. Below, a taxi turned a corner, its headlights sweeping across a wet intersection. Someone shouted somewhere in the distance, and someone else laughed.
Ash was quiet for a long time.
"To what?" he said finally. "The world didn't want me when it could see me."
She glanced at him. His face was turned away, but she could see the tension in his neck, the way the neon lines seemed to dim as if responding to his mood.
"That's not an answer," she said.

"It's the only one I've got." He shifted. The pipes in the HVAC unit behind him groaned. "I was a programmer before. Wrote code for a startup — nothing important, nothing that mattered. I was already invisible before the treatment made it literal. Long hours, empty apartment, one friend who stopped calling after the second month."
"The treatment?"

"ANX-00. The trial." His voice went flat. "Subject 03. They said it was for anxiety. Social disconnection. They said it would help me reintegrate." A sound that wasn't quite a laugh. "It worked, I guess. Now I'm more disconnected than ever."

Wren's pencil moved. She wasn't looking at the pad — she was looking at him. The set of his shoulders. The way his fingers curled loosely over his knees. The profile of his face against the city's glow, all harsh lines and hollows and that faint pulsing light.

She drew him the way she always drew him — quick, fierce strokes, barely looking at the page. Her pencil caught his jaw. The slope of his nose. The way his hair fell across his forehead, unkempt and dark.
He turned. Caught her.
His gaze dropped to the sketchpad. She froze, pencil mid-stroke.
"That's me," he said.
"Yeah."

He leaned closer. Not much. Just enough to see the page. The neon on his forearms cast a soft wash of blue-green light across the paper, illuminating the drawing — his own face staring back at him in graphite and shadow.
His expression didn't change. But something shifted behind it — a tremor, like a fault line slipping deep underground.
"That's the first time anyone's drawn me in three years," he said.
The words were simple. Matter-of-fact. But they landed in Wren's chest like a stone dropped into still water, sending ripples she couldn't control.

She thought about what it meant — three years without being seen, without being rendered, without existing in anyone's perception long enough to be captured. Three years of being a smudge, a blur, a gap in the visual field. And now here she was, holding a piece of paper with his face on it, proof that someone had looked at him long enough and close enough to know the shape of his scars.
"You could draw me too," she said, before she could think better of it. "If you wanted."
"I don't draw."
"What do you do?"

He looked at her. Really looked. The kind of look that made her skin prickle and her breath catch — not because it was gentle, but because it was total. Unblinking. The look of a man for whom observation was a survival skill turned into something more.

"I notice things," he said. "I notice that you tap your left ring finger when you're thinking. I notice you always sit with your back to the wall. I notice you draw faster when you're nervous and slower when you're sad."
Wren's left hand curled into a fist. She hadn't realized. She hadn't known anyone was paying that much attention.
"Three years of being invisible," he continued, "and you learn to watch. It's the only thing that keeps you sane — knowing that even if the world can't see you, you can still see it."
"That sounds lonely."
"Yeah." A beat. "It is."

They sat in silence. The city breathed below them. The neon on his arms pulsed steadily, a heartbeat made visible. Wren's pencil had stopped moving, but her hand still rested on the sketchpad, his face looking up at her from the page.
She wanted to say something. Something about how he wasn't alone anymore, how she saw him, how the drawing was proof.
But the words felt too big for her mouth, so she said nothing.

Ash leaned back against the HVAC unit. His shoulder was inches from hers. Not touching. Close enough that she could feel the warmth radiating off his skin, could hear the measured pace of his breathing.
They watched the city together — two ghosts at the edge of a world that had no room for them, sharing a silence that said more than speech ever could.
And when Wren finally closed her sketchpad and climbed back down into the dark, she caught him watching her go.
She didn't look away.
Neither did he.
